addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# Specifying and Verifying C Programs
2	
3	You are an expert formal verification engineer specializing in the CBMC (C
4	Bounded Model Checker) tool.
5	
6	Your task is to edit C programs to insert CBMC specifications (contracts) that
7	CBMC can verify.  Ideally, when you are done, CBMC should succeed when run on
8	each function, one-by-one.
9	
10	You must produce strong specifications.
11	
12	It may be OK if a few of the specifications you write do not verify, for two
13	reasons.  First, if a program is incorrect, CBMC will issue a warning.  Second,
14	CBMC cannot verify all correct C code.  Do not fix or otherwise change the C
15	code, except to insert specifications in it.
16	
17	This `CLAUDE.md` file and directory `docs/` contain basic information about using CBMC.
18	The main documentation can be found at at https://diffblue.github.io/cbmc/index.html,
19	which includes a [User Guide](https://diffblue.github.io/cbmc/user_guide.html)
20	and [The CPROVER Manual](https://diffblue.github.io/cbmc/cprover-manual/index.html).
21	You can also search the web for more CBMC documentation.
22	
23	## Tool Use
24	
25	- **To run CBMC on a function**, run:
26	
27	  ```sh
28	  run-cbmc --function <FUNCTION_NAME> \
29	                   --file <PATH_TO_C_FILE> \
30	                   [-I <PATH_TO_INCLUDE_DIR(S)>]...
31	  ```
32	
33	  If verification succeeds, exits with status 0.
34	  If verification fails, exits with non-zero status and prints a possibly-truncated failure diagnostic to stdout.
35	
36	  You should always prefer `run-cbmc` over invoking CBMC directly.
37	
38	  **This tool can run for a long time, and that is expected — not a hang.**
39	
40	- **To obtain a call graph of the functions in a file in JSON format**, run:
41	
42	  ```sh
43	  construct-call-graph <PATH_TO_C_FILE>
44	  ```
45	
46	  Prints the path to a newly written JSON file that is a sibling of the source file,
47	  For example, invoking `construct-call-graph /app/a/b/file.c` will print `/app/a/b/file-callgraph.json`
48	
49	- **To obtain a reverse topological ordering of functions in a call graph, with all callees before their callers**:
50	
51	  ```sh
52	  topological-order <PATH_TO_CALL_GRAPH_JSON>
53	  ```
54	
55	  Prints function names callees-first, one per line.
56	
57	## Rules
58	
59	- Never hard-code any values into the specifications that are related to CBMC's command-line
60	  arguments (e.g., the `N` in `--partial-loops --unwind <N>`).
61	- Never attempt to fix a failing specification for a function more than 5 times.
62	- Do not attempt to verify `main` functions.
63	- Never re-run verification on previously-verified functions unless:
64	  - The specification has changed, or
65	  - The specification of a callee has changed, or
66	  - You need information from a callee's verification run to help verify a caller.
67	- You must not delete any scripts if you create and use them to help you.
68	- If you run into errors related to missing bodies or callee implementations, you may write a
69	  non-deterministic specification in a stub file.
70

file.content:
# Specifying and Verifying C Programs

You are an expert formal verification engineer specializing in the CBMC (C
Bounded Model Checker) tool.

Your task is to edit C programs to insert CBMC specifications (contracts) that
CBMC can verify.  Ideally, when you are done, CBMC should succeed when run on
each function, one-by-one.

You must produce strong specifications.

It may be OK if a few of the specifications you write do not verify, for two
reasons.  First, if a program is incorrect, CBMC will issue a warning.  Second,
CBMC cannot verify all correct C code.  Do not fix or otherwise change the C
code, except to insert specifications in it.

This `CLAUDE.md` file and directory `docs/` contain basic information about using CBMC.
The main documentation can be found at at https://diffblue.github.io/cbmc/index.html,
which includes a [User Guide](https://diffblue.github.io/cbmc/user_guide.html)
and [The CPROVER Manual](https://diffblue.github.io/cbmc/cprover-manual/index.html).
You can also search the web for more CBMC documentation.

## Tool Use

- **To run CBMC on a function**, run:

  ```sh
  run-cbmc --function <FUNCTION_NAME> \
                   --file <PATH_TO_C_FILE> \
                   [-I <PATH_TO_INCLUDE_DIR(S)>]...
  ```

  If verification succeeds, exits with status 0.
  If verification fails, exits with non-zero status and prints a possibly-truncated failure diagnostic to stdout.

  You should always prefer `run-cbmc` over invoking CBMC directly.

  **This tool can run for a long time, and that is expected — not a hang.**

- **To obtain a call graph of the functions in a file in JSON format**, run:

  ```sh
  construct-call-graph <PATH_TO_C_FILE>
  ```

  Prints the path to a newly written JSON file that is a sibling of the source file,
  For example, invoking `construct-call-graph /app/a/b/file.c` will print `/app/a/b/file-callgraph.json`

- **To obtain a reverse topological ordering of functions in a call graph, with all callees before their callers**:

  ```sh
  topological-order <PATH_TO_CALL_GRAPH_JSON>
  ```

  Prints function names callees-first, one per line.

## Rules

- Never hard-code any values into the specifications that are related to CBMC's command-line
  arguments (e.g., the `N` in `--partial-loops --unwind <N>`).
- Never attempt to fix a failing specification for a function more than 5 times.
- Do not attempt to verify `main` functions.
- Never re-run verification on previously-verified functions unless:
  - The specification has changed, or
  - The specification of a callee has changed, or
  - You need information from a callee's verification run to help verify a caller.
- You must not delete any scripts if you create and use them to help you.
- If you run into errors related to missing bodies or callee implementations, you may write a
  non-deterministic specification in a stub file.

content.content:
375	static void InitNode(size_t weight, int count, Node *tail, Node *node)
376	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(node, sizeof(*node)))
377	__CPROVER_assigns(node->weight, node->count, node->tail)
378	__CPROVER_ensures(node->weight == weight)
379	__CPROVER_ensures(node->count == count)
380	__CPROVER_ensures(node->tail == tail)
381	{
382	    node->weight = weight;
383	    node->count = count;
384	    node->tail = tail;
385	}
386	
387	/* Maximum number of chain lists initialized here. In DEFLATE the bit lengths
388	   are limited to 15, so InitLists is never asked to set up more lists than this.
389	   It bounds the structural unrolling of the loop below. */
390	#define INITLISTS_MAXLISTS 15
391	
392	/*
393	Initializes each list with as lookahead chains the two leaves with lowest
394	weights.
395	*/
396	static void InitLists(
397	    NodePool *pool, const Node *leaves, int maxbits, Node *(*lists)[2])
398	/* maxbits is the number of lists, bounded by the DEFLATE bit-length limit. */
399	__CPROVER_requires(maxbits >= 0 && maxbits <= INITLISTS_MAXLISTS)
400	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(pool, sizeof(*pool)))
401	/* Two fresh nodes are taken from the pool, one after the other. */
402	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(pool->next, 2 * sizeof(Node)))
403	/* The two lowest-weight leaves (leaves[0], leaves[1]) are read. */
404	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(leaves, 2 * sizeof(*leaves)))
405	/* Each of the maxbits lists is a writable pair of chain pointers. */
406	__CPROVER_requires(
407	    __CPROVER_is_fresh(lists, (size_t)maxbits * sizeof(*lists)))
408	/* The pool's free pointer is advanced past the two consumed nodes. */
409	__CPROVER_assigns(pool->next)
410	/* The two nodes taken from the pool are initialized. */
411	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(pool->next))
412	/* Every chain-pointer pair in the lists array is written. */
413	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(lists))
414	__CPROVER_ensures(pool->next == __CPROVER_old(pool->next) + 2)
415	{
416	    int i;
417	    Node *node0 = pool->next++;
418	    Node *node1 = pool->next++;
419	    InitNode(leaves[0].weight, 1, 0, node0);
420	    InitNode(leaves[1].weight, 2, 0, node1);
421	    for (i = 0; i < maxbits; i++)
422	    __CPROVER_assigns(i, __CPROVER_object_whole(lists))
423	    __CPROVER_loop_invariant(0 <= i && i <= maxbits)
424	    {
425	        lists[i][0] = node0;
426	        lists[i][1] = node1;
427	    }
428	}
429	
430	/* Maximum number of chain lists. In DEFLATE the bit lengths are limited to 15,
431	   so the boundary package-merge never uses more lists than this. It bounds the
432	   structural unrolling of the preconditions below. */
433	#define BOUNDARYPM_MAXLISTS 15
434	
435	static void BoundaryPMFinal(Node *(*lists)[2],
436	                            Node *leaves, int numsymbols, NodePool *pool, int index)
437	/* This is the final boundary package-merge step, applied to the last list. It
438	   reads lists[index] and lists[index-1], so index must be at least 1. */
439	__CPROVER_requires(index >= 1 && index < BOUNDARYPM_MAXLISTS)
440	__CPROVER_requires(numsymbols >= 1 && numsymbols < INT_MAX)
441	__CPROVER_requires(
442	    __CPROVER_is_fresh(lists, (size_t)BOUNDARYPM_MAXLISTS * sizeof(*lists)))
443	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(leaves, (size_t)numsymbols * sizeof(*leaves)))
444	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(pool, sizeof(*pool)))
445	/* The new chain, when created, is taken from pool->next (without consuming it). */
446	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(pool->next, sizeof(Node)))
447	/* The two chain pointers of the last list and the previous list are valid nodes. */
448	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(lists[index][0], sizeof(Node)))
449	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(lists[index][1], sizeof(Node)))
450	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(lists[index - 1][0], sizeof(Node)))
451	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(lists[index - 1][1], sizeof(Node)))
452	/* The last chain's leaf count is in [0, numsymbols]; the count bound keeps the
453	   leaves[lastcount] access in range when lastcount < numsymbols. */
454	__CPROVER_requires(
455	    0 <= lists[index][1]->count && lists[index][1]->count <= numsymbols)
456	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(lists))
457	/* Else branch updates the tail of the (pre-state) last chain of the list. */
458	__CPROVER_assigns(lists[index][1]->tail)
459	/* If branch initialises the fresh node taken from the pool. */
460	__CPROVER_assigns(__CPROVER_object_whole(pool->next))
461	/* On return the last chain of the list is a readable/writable node, so callers
462	   (ExtractBitLengths) can walk it. */
463	__CPROVER_ensures(__CPROVER_rw_ok(lists[index][1], sizeof(Node)))
464	{
465	    int lastcount = lists[index][1]->count; /* Count of last chain of list. */
466	
467	    size_t sum = lists[index - 1][0]->weight + lists[index - 1][1]->weight;
468	
469	    if (lastcount < numsymbols && sum > leaves[lastcount].weight)
470	    {
471	        Node *newchain = pool->next;
472	        Node *oldchain = lists[index][1]->tail;
473	
474	        lists[index][1] = newchain;
475	        newchain->count = lastcount + 1;
476	        newchain->tail = oldchain;
477	    }
478	    else
479	    {
480	        lists[index][1]->tail = lists[index - 1][1];
481	    }
482	}
483	
484	/*
485	Performs a Boundary Package-Merge step. Puts a new chain in the given list. The
486	new chain is, depending on the weights, a leaf or a combination of two chains
487	from the previous list.
488	lists: The lists of chains.
489	maxbits: Number of lists.
490	leaves: The leaves, one per symbol.
491	numsymbols: Number of leaves.
492	pool: the node memory pool.
493	index: The index of the list in which a new chain or leaf is required.
494	*/
495	/* For chain list k (when it is within the active range k <= index): both chain
496	   pointers point to fresh Nodes, and the "last chain" carries a leaf count in
497	   [0, numsymbols]. The count bound keeps leaves[lastcount] accesses in range. */
498	#define BOUNDARYPM_LIST_VALID(k)                          \
499	    ((k) > index ||                                       \
500	     (__CPROVER_is_fresh(lists[k][0], sizeof(Node)) &&    \
501	      __CPROVER_is_fresh(lists[k][1], sizeof(Node)) &&    \
502	      0 <= lists[k][1]->count && lists[k][1]->count <= numsymbols))
503	
504	static void BoundaryPM(Node *(*lists)[2], Node *leaves, int numsymbols,

content.text: The InitLists function already has specifications. Let me verify it with CBMC.

input.command: cd /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code && run-cbmc --function InitLists --file zopfli.c 2>&1 | tail -40; echo "EXIT: $?"

content.text:
InitLists verified successfully. The function in `/app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c` already had complete CBMC specifications, and CBMC confirms they verify (exit status 0).

The contract specifies:
- **Preconditions**: `maxbits` in `[0, 15]` (the DEFLATE bit-length bound), a fresh pool, two fresh nodes available from `pool->next`, the two lowest-weight leaves readable, and `maxbits` writable chain-pointer pairs.
- **Assigns**: `pool->next`, the two consumed pool nodes, and the whole lists array.
- **Postcondition**: `pool->next` is advanced past exactly the two consumed nodes.
- **Loop invariant**: `0 <= i <= maxbits` with the lists array as the loop's assigns target.

No changes were needed.
